pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Adult-Onset Ornithine Transcarbamylase (OTC) Deficiency THE SMOKING GUN: Metabolic panel returned 24 hours after admission: Plasma Amino Acids revealed extremely low or undetectable Citrulline and Arginine. Urinary Organic Acids revealed dramatically elevated Orotic Acid. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: OTC is the most common urea cycle disorder (UCD), responsible for converting carbamoyl phosphate and ornithine to citrulline within the mitochondria of hepatocytes. While most cases present catastrophically in the neonatal period, partial enzyme activity mutations can remain asymptomatic until triggered in adulthood by significant metabolic stress (e.g., high protein load, surgery, viral illness, or prolonged fasting). In this case, the enzyme deficiency prevented the efficient detoxification of ammonia into urea. The unchecked accumulation of carbamoyl phosphate forces it to exit the mitochondria and enter the pyrimidine synthesis pathway, producing the tell-tale metabolic byproduct, Orotic Acid (the smoking gun). The hyperammonemia, acting as a neurotoxin, leads to astrocyte swelling (osmotic stress) and dysfunction, causing severe, rapid-onset cerebral edema, refractory seizures, and subsequent herniation—a true metabolic catastrophe requiring emergent ammonia scavenging and potentially continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) or hemodialysis for rapid ammonia clearance.
